channel.title: State-Policy Think-Tank Digest
channel.description: Latest posts from free-market policy institutes across the U.S.

item.title: [Beacontn] First Principles Series: Don’t Ban Data Centers. Don’t Subsidize Them, Either.
item.description: We can allow data centers to exist while keeping the courts open for nuisance lawsuits against bad actors who infringe upon protections like water rights when other property owners are actually harmed by them.

item.title: [Rifreedom] Un-Affordability: Speaker Blazejewski’s Budget Denies Science, Will Raise Energy Costs for Ocean Staters … but Center Claims One Major Victory
item.description:
Despite common-sense energy reforms suggested by Governor McKee; despite pledged reforms by all other New England states; and despite indisputable research that shows how Rhode Island’s net zero emissions energy strategy will lead to unaffordable costs and dangerous risks … the budget put forth by the new Speaker of the RI House of Representative, Christopher Blazejewski … doubles-down on the state’s radical Act On Climate mandates.

The post Un-Affordability: Speaker Blazejewski’s Budget Denies Science, Will Raise Energy Costs for Ocean Staters … but Center Claims One Major Victory appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Property Tax Relief Options Explained
item.description: The most important point to understand regarding property taxes is that they are budget-driven. This simply means local taxing districts, which include counties, cities, schools, fire districts, etc., set property tax budgets, and those budgets are the primary driver of property tax payments and the changes (usually increases) over time. Long-term property tax relief requires […]

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] Circuit Court – Adams County
item.description:
Circuit Court – Adams County

The post Circuit Court – Adams County appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Coverage vs. Care (Part 1)
item.description: On Valentine’s Day 1966, Gov. Bob Smylie called a special session of the state Legislature to deal, primarily, with reapportioning the Idaho Senate to comply with the U.S. Supreme Court’s one-man-one-vote requirement. Back then, there was a senator for each of the state’s 44 counties, and the courts had ruled the arrangement unconstitutional. So when […]

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells’ $368,885 Secret Meeting
item.description:
For Immediate Release: June 10, 2026 Contact: press@reforminggovernment.org   IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells $368,885 Secret Meeting Lawsuit argues DPI violated open meetings laws, seeks to invalidate DPI’s secret meeting that weakened Wisconsin testing standards Delafield, Wis. — The Institute for Reforming Government (IRG), represented by the Wisconsin Transparency Project, is taking the Department […]

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Maine’s 2026 Primary Shows Why RCV Should Be Repealed
item.description:
Maine voters went to the polls on June 9, but in several of the state’s most important primary races, Election Day did not actually produce a winner. Instead, both gubernatorial primaries and the Democratic primary in Maine’s 2nd Congressional District are headed into ranked-choice voting tabulation. As a result, Mainers are left waiting days after […]

The post Maine’s 2026 Primary Shows Why RCV Should Be Repealed appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.description:
By Naomi InmanTestimony by John A. Charles, Jr. Portland Public Schools briefly revisited...

item.title: [Jbartlett] Does Jeff Bezos benefit from a NH Business Enterprise Tax cut?
item.description:
New Hampshire’s unique state tax structure is hard to understand, especially for people who don’t own a business.  More than a third (36%) of the state General Fund and Education Trust Fund revenue comes from business taxes.  The vast majority of that comes from the Business Profits Tax (BPT), but the lesser-known Business Enterprise Tax […]

The post Does Jeff Bezos benefit from a NH Business Enterprise Tax cut? appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] Opinion piece: Report on economic development incentives is incomplete
item.description:
The following appeared in the Santa Fe New Mexican on June 6, 2026. It appeared in other papers across the state. Recently the Legislative Finance Committee produced a report in which (to summarize the findings) they made the case that New Mexico “spends” over half-a-billion dollars on tax breaks that generated little economic return for […]

The post Opinion piece: Report on economic development incentives is incomplete appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] API Launches Grassroots Push Urging Trump Administration to End the “Mail-Order Abortion Crime Scene” and Restore the In-Person Doctor’s Visit for Chemical Abortion Pills
item.description:
Alabama Policy Institute launched a grassroots effort calling on the Trump administration and members of Congress to end Joe Biden’s dangerous mail-order abortion scheme and restore the in-person doctor’s visit requirement that must be met before a woman can obtain chemical abortion drugs.

The post API Launches Grassroots Push Urging Trump Administration to End the “Mail-Order Abortion Crime Scene” and Restore the In-Person Doctor’s Visit for Chemical Abortion Pills appeared first on Alabama Policy Institute.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF on the road/post mortem on ABQ City Council victory
item.description:
This post will be a  lot less policy oriented than normal. That’s because as the head of RGF, this week has been VERY busy. It started with a HUGE win at ABQ City Council on Monday night when on a 5-4 vote Council (again) rejected a major tax increase. The vote was 5-4 against raising […]

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Stephanie Smith: Our children are not lab rats: Why we must restore parental authority in the digital age
item.description:
As a mother of seven children, I have spent decades learning how to navigate the beautiful, challenging, and sometimes exhausting work of raising the next generation. Today’s parents are facing an entirely different kind of battle than our parents did.

item.title: [Jbartlett] NH fishing & hunting license fees are among New England’s highest
item.description:
At the request of Gov. Kelly Ayotte, the New Hampshire Fish & Game Department has paused a proposed increase in fishing and hunting license fees.  Department officials say the increases are needed to shore up the budget. The last fee increase took effect in 2016.  A decade’s worth of inflation has eroded the value of […]

The post NH fishing & hunting license fees are among New England’s highest appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraska Population Growth: Are We Winning the Race for Residents?
item.description:
When measuring a state’s economic vitality, the most honest metric isn’t always in a government budget report—it’s in a moving van. Demographics are the bedrock of our workforce, tax base, and future prosperity. If we want to evaluate how Nebraska is truly performing, we have to look at whether we are attracting new talent or...

The post Nebraska Population Growth: Are We Winning the Race for Residents? appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Mspolicy] The Federal Scholarship Tax Credit – What You Need to Know
item.description:
Let me introduce you to three letters you are going to hear a great deal about in the months ahead: SGO. If they mean nothing to you today, they soon will. SGO stands for Scholarship Granting Organization — and thanks to a change in federal law, Mississippi is about to see a whole network of […]

The post The Federal Scholarship Tax Credit – What You Need to Know appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Portland Study Reinforces What We Already Know: Inclusionary Zoning is Terrible Policy
item.description:
For years, Maine Policy Institute has warned that Portland’s inclusionary zoning policy would make the city’s housing shortage worse, not better. Last December, when Portland commissioned yet another study on the subject, MPI noted that the evidence was already clear: Inclusionary zoning acts like a tax on housing development, raises the cost of building, and […]

The post Portland Study Reinforces What We Already Know: Inclusionary Zoning is Terrible Policy appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Affordability issue #1: Property tax increases far exceed most Kansans’ change in personal income
item.description: The #1 affordability issue facing many Kansans is property tax increases that are literally taxing some people out of their homes and prompting some small businesses to close. The average taxable assessed home value jumped 40% over the last four years, resulting in a 31% average property tax increase. Meanwhile, real (inflation-adjusted) per capita personal […]

item.title: [Rifreedom] ONE SCORE, Twenty Failures: The High Cost of Rhode Island’s One-Party Rule
item.description:
Rhode Island's twenty year single-party rule has driven sharp decline. The new “One Score” report details twenty major policy failures in taxes, affordability, education, business climate & more. Read it now.

item.description:

Ranking a solid 10th nationwide with a childcare freedom score of 5.48 out of 10, the Cornhusker State stands as a competitive leader in keeping childcare regulations manageable. Within the West North Central region, Nebraska ranks 2nd, demonstrating...

item.title: [Mspolicy] Government pays to lobby government — with your tax dollars
item.description:
Across Mississippi, government bodies spend public money hiring lobbyists — to lobby government. I would not have believed quite how much of it goes on until we built the tool that lays it bare. Let me explain how we got here. Within days of entering the White House in January 2025, President Trump set up […]

item.title: [Empowerms] Charter Schools Outperform Districts on 3rd Grade Reading Test Initial Results
item.description:
The Mississippi Department of Education (MDE) has released results of the initial administration of the 2026 third-grade reading assessment, and five of the six Mississippi charter schools with third-grade students outperformed their local school district. This assessment process is often referred to as the “Third Grade Gate” and is required under the state’s Literacy-Based Promotion…

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Coverage vs. Care (Part 2): What Was Lost and Why
item.description: Before Medicaid, Idahoans who needed medical care and lacked the means to pay for it relied on a number of alternatives: friends and family, charitable care from physicians, church networks, mutual aid societies, and community organizations that knew their neighbors by name. Fraternal orders — the Odd Fellows, the Elks, and the Knights of Columbus […]

by Randal O’Toole Affordable housing projects funded with Metro’s 2018 housing bond have...

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Washington state’s gas tax is going up in July and will automatically increase yearly; that’s a big problem
item.description: By automatically increasing gas taxes each year, Washington policymakers are ensuring voters will pay even more without the normal public accountability or public debate for a tax increase. This yearly automatic gas tax increase should be towed back to the legislative garage for major repairs.

item.title: [Beacontn] America at 250: Wins Worth Celebration
item.description: The wins we at Beacon celebrate are all grounded in the principles of freedom upon which our nation was founded.

item.title: [Jbartlett] Laconia beats Boston to launch first social district in New England
item.description:
On March 21, Laconia launched New England’s first social district, a designated area where drinking is allowed in public under tightly regulated conditions.  With Motorcycle Week looming, critics on social media mockingly predicted disaster.  A week later, Joia Hughes, Laconia’s economic development and housing director, summed up the damage. “No problems,” she said. Mayor Mike […]

The post Laconia beats Boston to launch first social district in New England appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Lawmakers agree suspended drivers on Wisconsin roads remain a problem
item.description:
While state law prohibits drivers from driving with a suspended license, it’s not a crime unless alcohol is involved.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Wisconsin is missing its Medicaid accountability moment
item.description:

item.description:

At the Maui event in Kahului, moderator and Grassroot President Keli‘i Akina said foremost among the victories were to help preserve most of […]

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Maui Council should heed recommendation to axe certain county boards
item.description:

Unfortunately, many of these volunteer-led boards do not have clear missions, have […]

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Will Swaim: America’s 250th and the Fight for Liberty in California
item.description:
When I told a friend recently that I was sorry to hear she was getting a divorce, she replied, “You wouldn’t be sorry if you really knew my husband.” When I admitted that I knew him but not really, she followed with a painfully detailed catalog of his offenses and promised that I’d get an...

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Cities Cannot Budget Their Way Out of Mission Problems
item.description:
California cities continue to struggle with recurring fiscal stress. Los Angeles recently closed a budget gap approaching $1 billion. San Jose addressed a shortfall of roughly $50 million while acknowledging that structural challenges remain. Oakland reopened its two-year budget to address a gap of roughly $265 million while continuing to wrestle with insolvency concerns. Across...

The post Cities Cannot Budget Their Way Out of Mission Problems first appeared on California Policy Center.

Over time, her passion for children, community, and meaningful learning experiences continued to grow. While working in schools, she began noticing something powerful: children often learned best when they were allowed to…

item.description: Last week, Yankee Institute proposed the Expenditure Records and Information Notification Act, or ERIN’s Act, a reform requiring executive branch agencies to publish purchasing-card transactions in a centralized, searchable online […]

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Who Decides Who Gets to Be a Lawyer in Louisiana?
item.description:
For more than four decades, a single private trade association in Chicago, the American Bar Association (ABA), has decided who is allowed to become a lawyer in nearly every state. That’s because nearly every state, including Louisiana, relies on the ABA to determine which law schools meet their standards. Almost all states require future lawyers...

The post Who Decides Who Gets to Be a Lawyer in Louisiana? appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF on with Bob Clark for TWO fantastic interviews
item.description:
On Thursday, June 25, 2026 RGF hosted a luncheon with speaker Tim Sandefur as part of the organization’s “America 250” celebrations. Tim signed copies of his book: “Proclaiming Liberty: John Adams, Thomas Jefferson, and the Declaration of Independence.” You can check out the interview Bob Clark of KKOB radio did with Tim below: The following day Paul […]

The post RGF on with Bob Clark for TWO fantastic interviews appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Inpolicy] Franke: The Declaration Parchment
item.description: by Mark Franke With the semiquincentennial of the Declaration of Independence, there is a plethora of new books written about this major event. Most focus on the Declaration’s language and its meaning, especially its most memorable sentence in the second paragraph. That’s understandable but I am a contrarian by nature so I have been wondering Read the full article...

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Iowa Climbs in National Competitiveness Rankings
item.description:
Iowa's pro-growth tax reforms are delivering results. Iowa climbed from 30th to 25th in the Rich States, Poor States rankings after enacting a 3.8% flat income tax, lowering corporate taxes, and implementing other taxpayer-friendly reforms that have strengthened the state's economy and competitiveness. The work isn't finished. While Iowa has made historic progress, other states are continuing to cut taxes even more aggressively. To remain competitive, Iowa must continue improving its tax climate rather than becoming complacent. Spending restraint is the key to lasting tax relief. Meaningful tax reform is only possible when government spending is controlled. Iowa's new 2% cap on property tax growth builds on that principle and helps lay the foundation for long-term fiscal responsibility.

The post Iowa Climbs in National Competitiveness Rankings appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] The legacy of the Declaration of Independence
item.description: Part of a citizen’s job is to inform the rising generation of what the Declaration really said, what it really means, and why it is important. May we continue that effort long after this 250th anniversary has receded into history.

item.description:
Fourteen retired New York City educators received more than $500,000 in pension benefits in 2025, according to data released by....

item.title: [Mspolicy] AI — Rocket Fuel for the Liberty Revolution
item.description:
Over the years, the liberty movement has won some remarkable victories. Think of how school choice has advanced (albeit not yet in Mississippi), or the wave of flat-tax reforms rolled out across many states. What makes this all the more impressive is that the change has been achieved with often limited resources.  Our opponents are […]

The post AI — Rocket Fuel for the Liberty Revolution appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] New A–F Grades for Kansas Schools Highlight Impact of Lowered Standards
item.description: The newest A–F Grading of Kansas Schools is here. A-F Grading provides Kansans with a clear, easy-to-understand snapshot of academic performance statewide. The A–F system translates complex state assessment data into familiar letter grades, offering a transparent alternative to Kansas’ official school report cards, which often rely on technical labels that are difficult for parents […]

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] When licensing goes too far
item.description:
I always enjoy the opportunity to close out the legislative session talking with the people that we at the Grassroot Institute of Hawaii are really working for — the men and women who are trying to live, work and flourish in Hawaii. It’s a great reminder that the bills we support or oppose have a […]

The post When licensing goes too far   appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Why High Energy Prices Will Plague Massachusetts for Years to Come
item.description:
Amid our recent harsh winter, Governor Maura Healey issued an immediate reduction of electricity bills by 25% across the state and a 10% gas bill reduction. However, these are temporary relief measures, as Massachusetts is forced to reconcile how it will address its aging infrastructure, fuel constraints, and the cost pressures placed on consumers.

Currently, Massachusetts’ retail electricity cost ranges from about $0.28-$0.32/kWh, which is about 40-60% higher than the US national average of $0.20/kWh. It is also a sharp increase from Massachusetts’ last recorded rate of $0.23/kWh in 2023, according to Pioneer’s DataLabs, which placed Massachusetts fourth highest nationally. Utilities company Eversource Energy has examined the increase in residential gas bill prices since 2015, breaking down bill components into four categories: maintenance and infrastructure, Gas System Enhancement Plan (GSEP), public benefits that support state-mandated programs (i.e. Mass Save), and supply. While each component has shown increases over the past ten years, public benefits rose 200% and supply related costs rose 175%.

The post Why High Energy Prices Will Plague Massachusetts for Years to Come appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] Yankee Institute’s 2026 Public Policy Toolkit
item.description: At Yankee Institute, we envision a Connecticut that is affordable, livable, and workable. Our 2026 Public Policy Toolkit advances that vision by explaining how policy decisions function in practice, making […]

item.description:
Date Filed: June 29, 2026 Original Court: Northern District of California Case Status: Pending Matthew Bernard and Lynn Warner own an undeveloped residential plot in Oakland, California. In 2020, the Oakland Fire Department ordered them to clear dead and dying trees from their land or face ongoing fines. The landowners hired an arborist and removed...

item.title: [Mackinac] ‘Vote No’ means vote no
item.description: Will Saginaw Township Community Schools finally listen to voters?

item.title: [Inpolicy] Eichenberger: The Linguistic Assault on Reality
item.description: by Dan Eichenberger M.D. Language remains one of humanity’s most powerful tools. Spoken words helped early humans cooperate, hunt and pass down knowledge. Written language allowed civilizations to record laws, ideas and stories for future generations. Today, language is under attack. The political Left, particularly progressive activists who dominate many colleges, news outlets and major corporations, Read the full article...

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] AI in Medicine: More Than a Hypothetical
item.description:
The average American is optimistic about how artificial intelligence (AI) can change healthcare. Anyone who has ever experienced or been near to a difficult health problem understands that the hope that a rapidly developing technology offers is not a small one. And while medical progress is often cited as one of the most valuable and...

The post AI in Medicine: More Than a Hypothetical appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Using AI to Reduce State Regulations
item.description:
Federal regulation has grown into something almost impossible to fully grasp. The U.S. Code of Federal Regulations now runs over 180,000 pages, compared to just 10,000 pages in 1950. States have not fared any better. Ohio's code recently surpassed 17 million words. At that scale, checking every rule against current law to see what is outdated or contradictory is not realistic. Therefore, almost nobody does, and the rules keep piling up.

In the past some states have attempted to arduously tackle the problem by hand. For example, former Massachusetts’ Governor Charlie Baker launched a "regulatory review" effort in 2015 that ran through 2017. Many other states have attempted similar reviews over the years. Reviewing an entire regulatory code, however, is slow and labor-intensive, and many of these earlier efforts ended up incomplete.

The post Using AI to Reduce State Regulations appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Homeowners Insurance Premiums Continues to Rise Amid Climate Risk
item.description:
Across the United States, annual homeowner insurance premiums have increased by an average of $648 from 2021 to 2024, according to the Consumer Federation of America. While there are widespread cost increases nationally, several communities have been hit harder than others due to the rise in severe weather events in the past few years. The states that saw the greatest percentage increase in premiums were Utah (59%), Illinois (50%), Arizona (48%), and Pennsylvania (44%). This contrasts with those states that saw the greatest hike in absolute dollars, which were Florida ($2,118), Louisiana ($1,775), and Kentucky ($1,426).

These two pieces of data tell us two things about the effect of climate risk on insurance premiums: new areas are being exposed to disasters and known high-risk areas are facing exacerbated impacts.

The post Homeowners Insurance Premiums Continues to Rise Amid Climate Risk appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] 250 Years On: What We Owe This Moment
item.description:
Raise a glass to freedom, something they can never take away. My favorite song from Hamilton has been going through my head nonstop all week (my second favorite week of the year, after Mardi Gras!). Every Independence Day, my family does what most Louisiana families do. We cook, we light things on fire, and we...

The post 250 Years On: What We Owe This Moment appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: Not a Traitor, Exactly DEMOCRATIC ‘REPRESENTATION’ has lost any meaning. This year, 90 percent of representatives to the U.S. House will be determined before a general election, that is, gerrymandering will allow our “representatives” to choose who they “represent.” Mulling this upside-down state of affairs, the question occurs: Can we even define who we are not? Read the full article...

item.title: [Itrfoundation] The Jungle (Primary) Will Bring You to Your Knees
item.description:
Iowa's primary system protects both voters and political parties while still allowing independents to participate. Any voter can change their party affiliation at the polls on primary day, ensuring access without allowing one party to influence another party's nominee. Jungle primaries and ranked-choice voting add confusion, not clarity. These systems can produce same-party general elections, ballot exhaustion, lengthy vote counts, and a more complicated election process for voters and election officials alike. Iowa's election system isn't broken and doesn't need a radical overhaul. Courts have upheld the constitutionality of closed primaries, and Iowa's straightforward process promotes election integrity, transparency, and confidence in the outcome.

The post The Jungle (Primary) Will Bring You to Your Knees appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] Politicians Say They Want Affordable Housing. So Why Are They Killing It?
item.description:
A troubling pattern is emerging across the American West: State and local bureaucracies are quietly reshaping water policy in the..

The post Politicians Say They Want Affordable Housing. So Why Are They Killing It? appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Takes DPI to Court Over $34,000 Transparency Tax
item.description:
WILL has filed a lawsuit against the Wisconsin Department of Public Instruction (DPI) for flagrantly violating the state's Open Records Law. After nearly a year of stonewalling, DPI is effectively hiding public records behind an illegal, exorbitant $34,014 price tag for electronic files the agency has already located.

The post WILL Takes DPI to Court Over $34,000 Transparency Tax appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] What Matters Most to You as a Teacher?
item.description:
Teachers' unions are among the most politically active organizations in the country. They endorse candidates, contribute to political causes, and advocate for policies on issues far beyond wages and working conditions. They’ve taken positions on school choice, immigration, LGBTQ issues, curriculum, library books, and other controversial topics.

The post What Matters Most to You as a Teacher? appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Empowerms] Andy Taggart: The Ideas That Made America
item.description:
Andy Taggart: The Ideas That Made America As America approaches its 250th anniversary, what exactly are we celebrating? In this episode, Grant sits down with attorney, writer, and student of history Andy Taggart to explore the ideas behind the Declaration of Independence and why they remain relevant nearly two and a half centuries later. They…

The post Andy Taggart: The Ideas That Made America first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Kansas Needs Growth, Not Managed Decline
item.description: The latest bad idea from the global policy class is dressed up as compassion: the world needs less growth. That may sell at international conferences. It should not sell in Kansas. In a recent piece for the American Institute for Economic Research’s The Daily Economy,“The Poverty of the UN’s Degrowth Agenda,” I explained why the push […]

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Gifted, then Stagnant: The Argument for Excellence Offline
item.description:
I was a pretty smart kid. Why can’t I figure this social media thing out? It’s 11:45 pm and I’ve been scrolling on my phone for hours. This is not an experience that is unique, and I imagine many of you have the same experience. Despite feeling tired, my mind is racing, stimulated by the...

The post Gifted, then Stagnant: The Argument for Excellence Offline first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraska’s Child Care Costs Are Holding Back Young Families
item.description:
We recently posted about childcare regulations, and Nebraska’s solid standing according to the Archbridge Institute. But there are lots of data points to consider when it comes to childcare issues, and how childcare influences a state’s workforce, and another one showed up today, from Wallethub–childcare costs. When we talk about Nebraska’s outmigration problem, we often...

The post Nebraska’s Child Care Costs Are Holding Back Young Families appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] The Faulty Logic of the Anti-School Choice Position
item.description:
Listen to this article The anti-school choice position is usually framed as a defense of public education. But at its core, what it really does is defend a particular way […]

The post The Faulty Logic of the Anti-School Choice Position appeared first on Show-Me Institute.

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Setting the Record Straight: The Truth About Federal Education Tax Credits
item.description:
The federal education tax credit, enacted as part of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act and structured as the Education Freedom Tax Credit, is a policy designed to expand school choice using private donations rather than direct government spending.

The post Setting the Record Straight: The Truth About Federal Education Tax Credits appeared first on Alabama Policy Institute.

item.title: [Mackinac] The subtle art of concealing corporate welfare payments
item.description: Hundreds of millions get transferred in secret and without budget approval

item.title: [Pacificresearch] H-2A bill looks to update the program for the better
item.description:
The H-2A farmworker visa program might finally be headed for the overhaul it has needed for a long time. The U.S. House of Representatives has an opportunity to take up the Securing Agriculture’s Workforce Act (SAWA) currently being championed by House Agriculture Chairman G.T. Thompson. Under the current program, farmworkers are limited to seasonal jobs […]

The post H-2A bill looks to update the program for the better appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Chicago one of only two big cities without mayoral, city council term limits
item.description:
A city charter could impose them. All other big cities have one

The post Chicago one of only two big cities without mayoral, city council term limits appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] NJPI: Largest Budget in State History Falls Short on Transparency and Fiscal Responsibility
item.description:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE   Parsippany, NJ – The New Jersey Policy Institute today expressed disappointment with the process surrounding the adoption […]

The post NJPI: Largest Budget in State History Falls Short on Transparency and Fiscal Responsibility first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] America 250: A republic, if we can keep it
item.description: Americans should not take the wisdom and thoughtful deliberation of the founders for granted, and we can’t be deceived into thinking that the American experiment can continue to thrive without the participation of an informed and engaged citizenry.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Where does the Money Austin Spends on Homelessness Go?
item.description:
Austin Reform has recently dug into where the money is going in the homeless industrial complex, since for the 10th year in a row, homelessness in Austin has increased. How has $1.5 billion been spent over six years with no success? According to the report, which you can read here: $35M | The Official City...

The post Where does the Money Austin Spends on Homelessness Go? first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Challenges Arrowhead School District for Religious Censorship
item.description:
WILL is demanding that the Arrowhead Union High School District remedy unconstitutional religious discrimination after school officials prohibited graduating senior Sarianne Beronja from displaying a Bible verse on her commencement slideshow.

The post WILL Challenges Arrowhead School District for Religious Censorship appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL v. DPI
item.description:
WILL has filed a lawsuit asking a judge to step in and compel the Department of Public Instruction (DPI) to immediately release records regarding denied educator licenses.

The post WILL v. DPI appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] Lamont Got the Endorsement. What Did Labor Get?
item.description: On June 26, Gov. Ned Lamont won the Connecticut AFL-CIO’s endorsement over Democratic primary challenger Josh Elliott following closed-door interviews with both candidates. The Connecticut AFL-CIO is the state’s largest […]

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] Biden-era Rules Are Putting Wokeness Over Science
item.description:
At the Metropolitan Museum of Art, even a flute can now be treated as human remains, as it indicated in..

The post Biden-era Rules Are Putting Wokeness Over Science appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Choosing the Right Infrastructure Projects Can Deliver Affordable Abundance
item.description:
The reason to socialize a portion of the cost of major infrastructure is so the ratepayer doesn’t have to bear an unaffordable share of the construction financing payments. The broader benefit to society is to achieve greater affordability for what constitutes raw materials for prosperity; transportation, water, and energy. Whether or not they are ever...

The post Choosing the Right Infrastructure Projects Can Deliver Affordable Abundance first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Sutherlandinstitute] Parents cut out of their children’s decisions will get a chance to make their case to the Supreme Court
item.description:
Governments should not interfere with parents' relationships with their children unless the parents are unfit. The Washington policy being challenged in this case turns that principle upside down. It assumes that certain parents are unfit without requiring any evidence that the charge is justified.

The post Parents cut out of their children’s decisions will get a chance to make their case to the Supreme Court appeared first on Sutherland Institute.

item.title: [Cardinalinstitute] Special Circumstance Reviews
item.description:
Special Circumstance Reviews Between 2023 and 2025, the West Virginia Board of Education launched nine Special Circumstance Reviews — structured state investigations into county school systems exhibiting signs of serious failure. These reviews, grounded in WV Code §18-2E-5 and WVBE Policy 2322, culminated in state takeovers of multiple districts. Taken together, they reveal not a […]

The post Special Circumstance Reviews appeared first on Cardinal Institute.

item.title: [Cardinalinstitute] Regulatory Reform in West Virginia
item.description:
Regulatory Reform in West Virginia The Problem: A State Buried in Red Tape West Virginia has accumulated one of the heaviest regulatory burdens in the nation. According to the State RegData project, the state now has 131,007 regulatory restrictions on the books — mandatory rules containing language like “shall,” “must,” and “may not” that impose […]

The post Regulatory Reform in West Virginia appeared first on Cardinal Institute.

item.title: [Cardinalinstitute] Charity Care
item.description:
Charity Care West Virginia non-profit hospitals enjoy billions in tax exemptions. In return, the social contract demands they provide meaningful charity care to struggling families. The data shows they are not holding up their end of the bargain. This brief examines the evidence — from IRS filings, Medicare cost reports, and federal subsidy data — […]

The post Charity Care appeared first on Cardinal Institute.

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] How Missouri Can Encourage Economic Growth
item.description:
In his 2026 report, Looking for Growth: A Productivity Story, economist Joseph Haslag finds that eliminating Missouri’s state income tax could raise the state’s annual growth rate by a quarter […]

The post How Missouri Can Encourage Economic Growth appeared first on Show-Me Institute.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] Florida Needs Workers to Build AI and Workers to Use It
item.description:
The demand for American workers who can build, power, and apply AI systems is outpacing supply.  This deficit...

The post Florida Needs Workers to Build AI and Workers to Use It appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] More Noise than Signal from Latest Chiefs Release
item.description:
Listen to this article The Kansas City Chiefs just released a two-page statement with numerous claims about the benefits of a new stadium, practice facility, and team headquarters in Kansas. […]

The post More Noise than Signal from Latest Chiefs Release appeared first on Show-Me Institute.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] A Tribute to The Honorable Daniel Oliver
item.description:
A Tribute to The Honorable Daniel Oliver Pacific Research Institute Chairman Emeritus and Board Member since 1991 The Honorable Daniel Oliver was a rare and remarkable man—steadfast in market-based principles and freedom, generous in spirit, and deeply devoted to the people and institutions he loved. For my husband Charles Kesler and me, Dan and his […]

The post A Tribute to The Honorable Daniel Oliver appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Sutherlandinstitute] U.S. Supreme Court agrees to hear two cases on parents’ rights and religious freedom, partly at Sutherland Institute’s urging
item.description:
The Supreme Court's decisions in both cases could provide important guidance to lower courts and public officials nationwide on the constitutional limits of government authority over families and religious practice.

The post U.S. Supreme Court agrees to hear two cases on parents’ rights and religious freedom, partly at Sutherland Institute’s urging appeared first on Sutherland Institute.

item.title: [Beacontn] Protected: The Declaration at 250: The Radical Idea That Made America
item.description: There is no excerpt because this is a protected post.

item.title: [Sutherlandinstitute] The Fourth of July is a reminder of America’s enduring promise
item.description:
Linking our official independence with the adoption of the Declaration is entirely fitting. The Declaration did much more than merely announce a political separation; it established an aspiration that shaped the new nation, and continues to shape us today.

The post The Fourth of July is a reminder of America’s enduring promise appeared first on Sutherland Institute.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] South Carolina Ends Legislation through Regulation
item.description:
When citizens challenge the government in court, they should expect a level playing field. They’ll get it now in South..

The post South Carolina Ends Legislation through Regulation appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Oregon’s largest teachers union prioritizing politics over education
item.description:
The Oregon Education Association has long claimed to speak for educators, students and public schools. But newly reported internal documents raise a fair question for Oregon teachers and education employees: Is OEA primarily focused on representing the workers who fund it or on growing its own political power?

The post Oregon’s largest teachers union prioritizing politics over education appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] Tale of two politicized social studies standards: Minnesota and Texas
item.description:
Both Minnesota and Texas are in the process of implementing new social studies standards that brought backlash for their politicized origin, alleged instructional bias, and sweeping changes. Besides the fact that Texas boasts the beginning of Interstate-35 and Minnesota the end, the two states don’t share much. While careful demographers in both states would argue...

The post Tale of two politicized social studies standards: Minnesota and Texas appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] Will Minnesota’s summer food program avoid another Feeding Our Future?
item.description:
Minnesota is once again administering a summer meal program that was exploited in the biggest fraud case connected to federal child nutrition programs. The Minnesota Department of Education (MDE) recently announced it will again run the federally funded Summer Food Service Program (SFSP), a U.S. Dept. of Agriculture (USDA) program that reimburses approved organizations for providing...

The post Will Minnesota’s summer food program avoid another Feeding Our Future? appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Record Month
item.description:
Every Fourth of July, Americans celebrate the ideals that founded this country — national sovereignty, individual liberty, self-determination and the right to make our own choices. At the Freedom Foundation, we see those principles in action every day. June was a record-breaking month for the organization, as 6,827 public employees exercised their rights and opted […]

The post Record Month appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] An Old-Fashioned Policy Solution
item.description:
The real driver of high taxes is high government spending. Lasting tax relief cannot be achieved by protecting government budgets or hoping for economic growth alone—it requires policymakers to control spending. Economic growth matters, but it is not a substitute for fiscal discipline. When government spending grows faster than the economy, taxpayers face increasing pressure and higher taxes that ultimately discourage growth. Minnesota Governor Theodore Christianson understood a timeless truth: the only sustainable way to reduce taxes is to spend less. His century-old message remains just as relevant for policymakers today.

The post An Old-Fashioned Policy Solution appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] While Minneapolis Bans Data Centers, It Greenlights Commercial Sex Venues — And Homeowners Pay the Price
item.description:
At a time when Minneapolis is shifting its tax burden from fleeing businesses to homeowners, you would think the city council would avoid the obvious contrast. But sadly, there is no such thing as embarrassment in the City of Lakes.

The post While Minneapolis Bans Data Centers, It Greenlights Commercial Sex Venues — And Homeowners Pay the Price appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Left-wing NYC congressional candidates prompt concerns
item.description:
Jon Levine writes for the Washington Free Beacon about the reaction to recent election results in the nation’s largest city. Democrats in New York City and beyond remain wary and far from sold on a trio of far-left candidates who swept city primaries last week, two of whom knocked out establishment Democratic incumbents. The New...

The post Left-wing NYC congressional candidates prompt concerns appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Critic laments ‘cowardice and incoherence’ of Roberts Court
item.description:
M.D. Kittle of the Federalist critiques the US Supreme Court under Chief Justice John Roberts. In his brilliant dissenting opinion eviscerating the majority’s legal gymnastics on birthplace citizenship, Justice Samuel Alito warned the court not to “adopt an erroneous interpretation of the Fourteenth Amendment simply out of fear of the consequences of ‘rocking the boat’ or...

The post Critic laments ‘cowardice and incoherence’ of Roberts Court appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Thomas says SCOTUS ruling ‘devalues’ being an American
item.description:
Randy DeSoto writes for the Western Journal about US Supreme Court Justice Clarence Thomas’ recent criticism of his colleagues. Justice Clarence Thomas, in a lengthy dissent, took the majority to task for interpreting the Constitution to mean that citizenship is automatically conferred on anyone born in the United States, regardless of the legal status of...

The post Thomas says SCOTUS ruling ‘devalues’ being an American appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] Michigan taxpayers are funding the push for higher taxes
item.description: School districts promote supposed benefits of spending measures

item.title: [Pacificresearch] E-bikes are fun, but cities need to enforce reasonable traffic laws
item.description:
On a fine October day in 2024 I was walking my Pomeranian show dog, Ollie, to my favorite Irvine coffee shop, the Lost Bean on Barranca Parkway. Suddenly I was on the ground. Two youths on e-bikes were riding on the sidewalk at maybe 25 mph and one cut in between Ollie and me. The […]

The post E-bikes are fun, but cities need to enforce reasonable traffic laws appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] America 250: Illinoisans show the promise of liberty
item.description:
State leaders can take the lead of businesses and residents and work to lessen the burden of corruption and high taxes.

The post America 250: Illinoisans show the promise of liberty appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Dozens of amendments hit lawmakers in last days of session
item.description:
Illinois lawmakers got many amendments at the end of the legislative session.

The post Dozens of amendments hit lawmakers in last days of session appeared first on Illinois Policy.
